reasoning:
The reference class is Brazilian incumbent presidents officially running for re-election. Every incumbent who has run for re-election has advanced to the runoff (or won outright). The Workers' Party (PT) also has an extraordinarily high floor, having placed in the top two of every presidential election since 1989. For Lula to miss the runoff, he would either have to withdraw entirely or suffer an unprecedented political collapse that drops him behind a moderate third-place candidate.

Currently, April 2026 polls (Datafolha, Quaest) show Lula easily clearing the rest of the field, effectively tied with right-wing challenger Flávio Bolsonaro in prompted first-round and runoff scenarios, and running far ahead of any third candidate like Tarcísio de Freitas (who polls in the single digits). He has officially declared his candidacy and recently appointed new ministers to bolster his campaign.

The only significant negative factor is his age (80) and recent medical history, which includes a December 2024 emergency surgery for a brain bleed (caused by a fall) and a January 2026 cataract surgery. He passed routine evaluations in March 2026 and remains highly active. However, an 80-year-old candidate carries a ~15-20% baseline risk of a severe health event or precautionary withdrawal over a grueling 6-month campaign. Polymarket traders currently price his chances at ~78.5%. Balancing his commanding poll numbers (which justify a >95% chance of advancing if he stays in the race) against a ~15-20% dropout/health risk, a ~79% probability is appropriate.
